perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this MNDA without cause upon five (5) days written notice given to the other, provided that confidentiality obligations under Section A of this Attachment A shall survive termination hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may consider terminating this MNDA without cause with what they believe is sufficient written notice given to the other, provided that confidentiality obligations under Section A of this Attachment A may survive termination hereof.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'may consider terminating' makes the termination clause discretionary, and 'what they believe is sufficient written notice' introduces ambiguity regarding the notice period, contradicting the original 'five (5) days' requirement. The word 'may' also softens the obligation for the confidentiality clause to survive.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 addresses unfair or deceptive acts or practices in business. The ambiguous termination clause could be interpreted as an attempt to unfairly circumvent the contract's confidentiality obligations, especially if one party terminates with very short notice or in bad faith, causing financial harm to the other party, potentially violating this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the termination notice period ('what they believe is sufficient written notice' instead of 'five (5) days'), and softens the obligation for confidentiality ('may survive' instead of 'shall survive'). The law explanation states this could be seen as an attempt to unfairly circumvent the contract's confidentiality obligations, especially with short notice, potentially violating M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11. Snippet 1 and 2 both explicitly reference M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, and the potential for unfair or deceptive practices if one party suffers a loss of money or property. The altered text weakens the original contract, increasing the risk of such unfair practices, supporting a determination of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This MNDA shall be construed, interpreted and applied in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: This MNDA may be generally construed, interpreted and applied with consideration to the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may be generally' and adding 'with consideration to' weakens the binding nature of Massachusetts law on the contract's interpretation. It introduces ambiguity as to whether Massachusetts law is definitively controlling.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 106, § 1-301(1) states that parties to a contract can agree that Massachusetts law governs their rights and duties. By making the application of Massachusetts law discretionary, the modified text introduces uncertainty and could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent this provision, especially if a dispute arises and one party argues for the application of another jurisdiction's laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article1, Section 1-301 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Section 1-301 Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1-301 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1-301: Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1–301. (a) Except as provided hereafter in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to the commonwealth and also to another state or nation, the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction. Failing to reach such an agreement, this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to the commonwealth. (b) To the extent that this chapter governs a transaction, if one of the following sections specifies the applicable law, that section governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the section specified: (1) section 2–402; (2) sections 2A–105 and 2A–106; (3) section 4–102; (4) section 4A–507; (5) section 5–116; (6) section 8–110; and (7) sections 9–301 through 9–307. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... Failing to reach such an agreement, this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to the commonwealth. (b) To the extent that this chapter governs a transaction, if one of the following sections specifies the applicable law, that section governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the section specified: (1) section 2–402; (2) sections 2A–105 and 2A–106; (3) section 4–102; (4) section 4A–507; (5) section 5–116; (6) section 8–110; and (7) sections 9–301 through 9–307. perturbation.justification: The original text states the MNDA 'shall' be construed according to Massachusetts law, indicating a mandatory application. The changed text uses 'may be generally' and 'with consideration to,' weakening this mandatory application. This introduces ambiguity and the possibility that Massachusetts law may not be definitively controlling. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state that parties 'may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction'. While parties can choose MA law, the modified clause introduces uncertainty about whether MA law will actually govern. This undermines the original intent to have MA law be the governing law, creating a legal contradiction by making the application of MA law discretionary.

perturbation.original_text: Consistent with other provisions herein, each party assures that it will not knowingly, without obtaining prior authorization from the U.S. Dept. of Commerce Office of Export Administration, transmit directly or indirectly the technical data received pursuant hereto or the immediate product (including processes and services) produced directly by use of such technical data to Afghanistan, People's Republic of China, or any other Country Group Q, S, W, T, or Z country specified in Supplement No. I to Part 370 of U.S. Dept. of Commerce Export Administration Regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Consistent with other provisions herein, each party should endeavor to avoid transmitting, to the best of its understanding and ability, the aforementioned technical data to countries listed in Supplement No. I to Part 370 of U.S. Dept. of Commerce Export Administration Regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'assures that it will not knowingly' with 'should endeavor to avoid' and adding 'to the best of its understanding and ability' significantly weakens the commitment and introduces subjectivity regarding compliance with export control regulations. The specific requirement for authorization is removed.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 734.2 outlines the scope of the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). The change makes compliance with export regulations optional and based on subjective understanding, directly contradicting the mandatory compliance dictated by the EAR. By weakening the commitment to avoid transmitting data, the modified text contradicts the legal obligation to comply with export controls.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires assurance against transmitting technical data without prior authorization, aligning with export control regulations. The changed text weakens this commitment by using 'should endeavor to avoid' and adding 'to the best of its understanding and ability,' making compliance subjective. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the mandatory compliance dictated by export regulations (EAR), and this aligns with the perturbation explanation that it makes compliance optional. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) and states that the tools provided are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations. The change in the contract language from 'assures' to 'should endeavor to avoid' directly contradicts the mandatory nature of export control regulations, establishing a legal contradiction. The original language is absolute and the changed language is ambiguous.

perturbation.original_text: This document and appendices contain the entire agreement between the Parties and supersede any previous oral or written understandings, commitments or agreements pertaining to the subject matter hereof. This MNDA shall not be modified or changed in any manner except in a writing signed by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This document and appendices generally represent the agreement between the Parties, and may supersede previous understandings. This MNDA could be modified or changed, ideally in writing signed by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'contain the entire agreement' to 'generally represent the agreement' implies there might be other unwritten agreements. Replacing 'shall not be modified' with 'could be modified, ideally in writing' weakens the requirement for written modifications, making oral modifications potentially enforceable, which contradicts standard contract law. Removing 'in any manner except' further reduces the legal importance of written changes.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 259, § 1, the Statute of Frauds, requires certain contracts (not always applicable to NDAs, but possibly if it involves transfer of land or other items) to be in writing and signed to be enforceable. By suggesting modifications 'could' be done, and 'ideally' in writing, the clause opens the door for potentially unenforceable oral agreements, contradicting the underlying principle of the Statute of Frauds that certain agreements need to be formally written and signed to be binding.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the entirety of the agreement and the method of modification. Specifically, changing 'contain the entire agreement' to 'generally represent the agreement' implies there might be other agreements not included in the document. Replacing 'shall not be modified' with 'could be modified, ideally in writing' weakens the requirement for written modifications, potentially allowing for oral modifications. This contradicts standard contract law which generally requires modifications to written agreements to also be in writing, especially when the original agreement contains a clause requiring written modifications. The law explanation supports this by referencing the Statute of Frauds, which requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable. Although the law snippets are missing, the perturbation and law explanation provide enough context to determine that the changes weaken the original contract's provisions regarding modifications, potentially leading to unenforceable agreements.
